text: Joe Duffy: <https://github.com/joeduffy/joeduffy.github.io/blob/master/_posts/2015-11-10-objects-as-secure-capabilities.md>

elements.text: Joe Duffy:

text: The idea is that instead of programs having permissions, permissions are _first class values_ that are passed to the program or supplied at runtime, such as via a OS file picker. These capabilities are then passed around in the program via function arguments, and they can be wrapped etc. to form smaller capabilities, thereby affording a very natural and granular security model.

elements.text: The idea is that instead of programs having permissions, permissions are
first class values

that are passed to the program or supplied at runtime, such as via a OS file picker. These capabilities are then passed around in the program via function arguments, and they can be wrapped etc. to form smaller capabilities, thereby affording a very natural and granular security model.

text:
In some sense, it's already a very popular model today, via _dependency injection_, where you explicitly pass in specific services that some code needs, rather than having that functionality gloabally available. Object capabilities essentially takes this programming
 pattern to its logical conclusion, and uses it as the security model as well.

elements.text: In some sense, it's already a very popular model today, via
dependency injection

, where you explicitly pass in specific services that some code needs, rather than having that functionality gloabally available. Object capabilities essentially takes this programming
 pattern to its logical conclusion, and uses it as the security model as well.

text: To take Log4Shell as a recent example, you might become suspecious if your logging framework explicitly asks for the capability to download and execute code. But when it just implicitly has those capabilities, nobody bats an eye.

elements.text: To take Log4Shell as a recent example, you might become suspecious if your logging framework explicitly asks for the capability to download and execute code. But when it just implicitly has those capabilities, nobody bats an eye.

text: Ah, OK, I'm still waiting for my Ah-ha! moment on this... I get the ACL Bad part now, just struggling with the Capabilities Good, or even the What are They?! So tokens for a permission for, the bearer? to do something?

text: Yes, you can view them as unforgable tokens that lets you do specific things, such as reading a certain file.

elements.text: Yes, you can view them as unforgable tokens that lets you do specific things, such as reading a certain file.

text: Is "you" the bearer in the code or is "you" a type of thing in the system, presumably it's not the user?

elements.text: Is "you" the bearer in the code or is "you" a type of thing in the system, presumably it's not the user?

text: Apologies to side track your OP, btw :grin:

text: Here's a code example. You'll have to excuse I'm using my own language Firefly here. In Firefly, `main` receives `System`, which is an object representing access to "everything". It contains other capabilities, such as the `FileSystem` , which represents access to the file system. You can further wrap such capabilities yourself to narrow them down, e.g. to only allow access to files in a particular directory.

elements.text: Here's a code example. You'll have to excuse I'm using my own language Firefly here. In Firefly,
main
receives
System
, which is an object representing access to "everything". It contains other capabilities, such as the
FileSystem
, which represents access to the file system. You can further wrap such capabilities yourself to narrow them down, e.g. to only allow access to files in a particular directory.

text: Here `deleteDirectory` is able to delete files because it's passed a `FileSystem`, whereas `parsePackageLocation` can do no such thing, since it isn't passed any capabilities.

elements.text: Here
deleteDirectory
is able to delete files because it's passed a
FileSystem
, whereas
parsePackageLocation
can do no such thing, since it isn't passed any capabilities.

text: OK, so still, what is the agent that has the capability, that's checked when the filesystem is asked to do something? Is it simply the bearer, or is the token cross-checked to a user, or to an executable?

elements.text: OK, so still, what is the agent that has the capability, that's checked when the filesystem is asked to do something? Is it simply the bearer, or is the token cross-checked to a user, or to an executable?

text: Also, without decompiling this program, you don't know it isn't deleting all your files, assuming "System" is actually all the user's permissions

elements.text: Also, without decompiling this program, you don't know it isn't deleting all your files, assuming "System" is actually all the user's permissions

text: The token is an imperfect analogy in this case. It's simply an object that's able to access the file system. The constructor is private, which is how the language enforces that it's unforgable (togehter with a strict type system).

elements.text: The token is an imperfect analogy in this case. It's simply an object that's able to access the file system. The constructor is private, which is how the language enforces that it's unforgable (togehter with a strict type system).

text: That is because it is easy to build demos.    Working tools that do the right thing in the general case are really hard.

elements.text: That is because it is easy to build demos.    Working tools that do the right thing in the general case are really hard.

text: Yes, that's right - so Firefly only uses object capabilities internally to protect against exploits from e.g. dependencies. You can go further if you build a whole operating system around this.

elements.text: Yes, that's right - so Firefly only uses object capabilities internally to protect against exploits from e.g. dependencies. You can go further if you build a whole operating system around this.

text: To some degree, browsers and phones employ a form of object capabilities when it comes to accessing the users files or devices.

elements.text: To some degree, browsers and phones employ a form of object capabilities when it comes to accessing the users files or devices.

text: I was going to ask you about Android's quite fine-grained permission model

elements.text: I was going to ask you about Android's quite fine-grained permission model

text: For example, the File object in JS represents read-only access to a single file: <https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/File>

elements.text: For example, the File object in JS represents read-only access to a single file:

text:
An object capability, conceptually, is pretty much a pointer that is (a) unforgeable (no casting integers or whatever) and (b) revokable.

If you're doing this at the OS level, the process would be given some capabilities at startup or via IPC. The kernel will keep track of what capabilities a process has and what they're linked to. IIUC it's also keeping track of capabilities that point to the process, e.g. cases where the process wants to offer an IPC interface to other processes; you can think of the those as virtual objects hosted in the process.

So yeah, pretty much bearer tokens. The idea is that a "user" only ever possesses capabilities to files they're allowed to delete, so they can only ever pass those files to the delete command. (by this philosophy, I find the idea of a System capability dubious; it sort of works if it's just your main function not trusting its libraries and your language/runtime is fully reference secure and you've disabled opening files by name, but why settle?).

elements.text:
An object capability, conceptually, is pretty much a pointer that is (a) unforgeable (no casting integers or whatever) and (b) revokable.

If you're doing this at the OS level, the process would be given some capabilities at startup or via IPC. The kernel will keep track of what capabilities a process has and what they're linked to. IIUC it's also keeping track of capabilities that point to the process, e.g. cases where the process wants to offer an IPC interface to other processes; you can think of the those as virtual objects hosted in the process.

So yeah, pretty much bearer tokens. The idea is that a "user" only ever possesses capabilities to files they're allowed to delete, so they can only ever pass those files to the delete command. (by this philosophy, I find the idea of a System capability dubious; it sort of works if it's just your main function not trusting its libraries and your language/runtime is fully reference secure and you've disabled opening files by name, but why settle?).

text: I agree. The answer to "why settle" is to be part of the near future rather than the far future. It's simply a tradeoff to fit into the existing popular operating systems.

elements.text: I agree. The answer to "why settle" is to be part of the near future rather than the far future. It's simply a tradeoff to fit into the existing popular operating systems.

text: Yes, it was a rhetorical "why settle".

elements.text: Yes, it was a rhetorical "why settle".

text: This is a rather startling post, from end-to-end.

elements.text: This is a rather startling post, from end-to-end.

text:
I liked this part:
> Darklang's major advantage remains the same: the holistic or integrated nature of the platform, language, editor, and infrastructure. Darklang's major features: <https://blog.darklang.com/how-dark-deploys-code-in-50ms/|Deployless>, Invisible Infrastructure, and Trace-Driven Development, were all difficult before Dark, because they occurred at the intersection points of the editor, language and infra. By removing that intersection in Darklang, these features were very obvious and fell out fairly naturally.
> 
> What are the intersection points in AI-generated code? Well, look at what <https://github.com/features/preview/copilot-x?ref=blog.darklang.com|Copilot> is building: Copilot Chat in the IDE, Copilot for Pull Requests, Copilot for Docs, and Copilot-cli. Super exciting, but those seem like 4 different products, meaning a lot of intersection points with existing workflows, code bases and production deployments which we have the potential to remove.

elements.text: I liked this part:
Darklang's major advantage remains the same: the holistic or integrated nature of the platform, language, editor, and infrastructure. Darklang's major features:
Deployless

, Invisible Infrastructure, and Trace-Driven Development, were all difficult before Dark, because they occurred at the intersection points of the editor, language and infra. By removing that intersection in Darklang, these features were very obvious and fell out fairly naturally.

What are the intersection points in AI-generated code? Well, look at what
Copilot

is building: Copilot Chat in the IDE, Copilot for Pull Requests, Copilot for Docs, and Copilot-cli. Super exciting, but those seem like 4 different products, meaning a lot of intersection points with existing workflows, code bases and production deployments which we have the potential to remove.

text: What might an AI-first environment look like? DarkLang mentions "the holistic or integrated nature of the platform, language, editor, and infrastructure." This feels right, because AI that can instrument *all* of these pieces will be more effective and helpful.

elements.text: What might an AI-first environment look like? DarkLang mentions "the holistic or integrated nature of the platform, language, editor, and infrastructure." This feels right, because AI that can instrument
all
of these pieces will be more effective and helpful.

text:
I meant it as, "something useful to me in my life."

It's true that not everything is easy. I'm still far away from reproducing <https://www.inkandswitch.com/potluck> for myself: <https://merveilles.town/@akkartik/109293317013542368>

But I'll get there. More generally, I think "working product for myself" is an excellent intermediate step between "demo" and "multitenant product". And going by <https://futureofcoding.slack.com/archives/CLYCGTCPL/p1680027205608979?thread_ts=1679949636.443929&cid=CLYCGTCPL>, it's possible that's all we need.
